title: Monoon oblongifolium
intro: Monoon oblongifolium, commonly known as the Long-leaf Monoon, is a plant species native to the tropical regions of Southeast Asia, belonging to the family Annonaceae. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions, due to its reported anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antifungal properties. The bioactive constituents of Monoon oblongifolium include annonaceous acetogenins, which have been shown to exhibit potent cytotoxic and anti-cancer activities. In traditional herbal medicine, Monoon oblongifolium is commonly prepared as a decoction or an infusion, as well as being used in the production of traditional remedies such as tonics and ointments.
taxonomy: Monoon oblongifolium is classified within the family Annonaceae. This family is commonly known as the custard-apple family. The Annonaceae family is a large and diverse group of flowering plants, with over 2,000 species. Monoon oblongifolium is a species of flowering plant that is native to tropical regions. It belongs to the genus Monoon, which is a group of evergreen trees and shrubs.

side_effects: Monoon oblongifolium, although commonly used in traditional medicine, can cause adverse reactions such as allergic contact dermatitis in some individuals. Other side effects may include gastrointestinal issues, such as diarrhea, stomach pain, and nausea. Moreover, the use of Monoon oblongifolium may interact with certain medications, like blood thinners and diabetes medications. There is limited scientific research on the long-term effects of Monoon oblongifolium, and more studies are needed to determine its safety and efficacy.
